input_path: data\papers\Ramdhan et al. - 2025 - Engineering Automotive Digital Twins on Standardized Architectures A Case Study.pdf

extracted_characteristics.system_under_study: The physical twin is a 1/10‑scale autonomous, teleoperated vehicle that has been retrofitted with advanced sensor and control technology. Its hardware hierarchy consists of lower‑level and upper‑level chassis structures plus dedicated autonomy elements (sensors, actuators, and ECU). The vehicle operates in a real‑world driving domain performing Adaptive Cruise Control (ACC) functions, requiring on‑board ECUs that are resource‑constrained, making it a suitable candidate for off‑loading computation to a digital twin.
extracted_characteristics.dt_services: The Digital Twin provides three core services: (1) Data Monitoring – a graphical user interface (UI) displays real‑time vehicle state data, projected driving path, and detected object tracks; it also allows users to view, save, and generate reports of vehicle data. (2) Computation Offloading – the ACC algorithm (lead‑vehicle detection, distance calculation, desired‑speed computation) is executed on the DT (e.g., cloud resources) and the resulting desired speed is sent back to the vehicle for actuation. (3) Remote Management – the UI enables operators to enable/disable ACC, set a desired speed, and issue an emergency‑braking command remotely. Target users are operators (who monitor and control ACC) and developers (who use the monitoring and data‑collection capabilities for troubleshooting). Service types include monitoring, visualization, control, and off‑loading of computation.
extracted_characteristics.tooling_and_enablers: Implementation relies on a low‑latency end‑to‑end communication link between vehicle and DT, cloud computing resources for off‑loaded ACC processing, a user interface (UI) for visualization and command input, and middleware components that bind the DT Entity with physical and software layers. No specific software platform or simulation engine is named in the document.
extracted_characteristics.twinning_time_scale: The DT requires that the physical twin and its digital counterpart share a common notion of time; clock synchronization must achieve at least 100 ms accuracy, which is the limit imposed by the available hardware. This synchronization constraint is required for computation off‑loading between devices; stricter sub‑100 ms bounds are mandated by external safety‑critical vehicular standards but are not imposed in this implementation.
extracted_characteristics.multiplicities: The system instantiates a single instance of each ISO‑23247 entity (Physical Twin Entity, Digital Twin Entity, User Entity). Each entity is deployed on a distinct hardware component, resulting in a decentralized distribution of functionality. Within the User Entity the User Interface FE is split into two sub‑FEs (visualization and control panel). No hierarchical nesting of multiple twin instances is described.
extracted_characteristics.dt_constellation: The architecture follows the ISO‑23247 reference architecture with an entity‑based decomposition. Functional Entities are realized as ROS 2 packages; each package contains one or more ROS 2 nodes that implement the FE’s behavior. The three entities communicate via ROS 2 middleware, providing data management, actuation and user interaction. Resource management is achieved by mapping each entity to dedicated compute resources (e.g., separate on‑board computers). The overall orchestration relies on ROS 2’s publish/subscribe and service mechanisms to coordinate the entities.
extracted_characteristics.physical_to_virtual_interaction: Sensor data (LiDAR point clouds) and vehicle state signals (motor, steering, servo, vehicle state) are streamed from the physical twin to the Digital Twin System (DTS). The raw point‑cloud stream is first processed by the Point Cloud Clustering & Tracking software component, after which the Data Translation functional entity (ISO 23247 Data Translation FE) serialises the data, aligns message types and performs unit conversions. Inter‑entity communication (physical ↔ digital) uses a Wi‑Fi link with UDP as the transport protocol, while intra‑DTS component communication takes place on a ROS 2 network. The processed and translated data are then fed to the Data Monitoring service and stored for reporting.
extracted_characteristics.virtual_to_physical_interaction: The DTS sends control commands back to the vehicle over the same Wi‑Fi/UDP link. Commands include ACC enable/disable, desired speed set‑point, and emergency‑brake activation. These commands are generated by the Controlling/Actuation functional entities, serialized by the Data Translation component, and transmitted to the vehicle’s actuators (motor, steering servo). The vehicle returns updated state information, which is displayed in the UI to confirm execution.
extracted_characteristics.dt_technical_connection: The technical connection consists of a ROS 2 middleware backbone for internal component messaging and a Wi‑Fi network using UDP for external communication between the physical twin and the DTS. The architecture follows a client‑server style where the vehicle acts as a data source and command receiver, while the DTS provides processing and UI services. The system is required to sustain low end‑to‑end latency to meet safety‑critical needs, though exact bandwidth or latency figures are not stated. Redundancy and security mechanisms are not detailed in the documents.
extracted_characteristics.dt_hosting_deployment: The DT services can be hosted on compute resources that may reside in the cloud, as the context mentions off‑loading compute to 'better‑suited equipment, e.g., in the cloud'. No specific details on storage size, scaling strategy, deployment models (container, VM, edge) or availability guarantees are provided.
extracted_characteristics.standardization: The Digital Twin complies with ISO 23247 (2021), the 'Digital twin framework for manufacturing' standard. Compliance involves adhering to Part 2 of the standard, which defines a reference architecture based on entities such as the Observable Manufacturing Element (OME), the Data Collection and Device Control Entity, the Digital Twin Entity, and the User Entity. Each entity is composed of Functional Entities that perform specific tasks (e.g., actuation, data transformation, user interfacing). The standard does not prescribe specific implementation technologies, but the architecture may be realized with protocols such as OPC Unified Architecture. No additional certification or interoperability specifications are described in the document.

block_1_docs_retrieved:
_A. Context_


DT technology enables an array of benefits for ACC through
off-loading any critical or non-critical computation for which
resource-constrained electronic control units (ECU) are typically not sufficient. To implement the ACC-DT, we develop
the following three services through a DT.
_1) Data Monitoring:_ Monitoring ACC-relevant data flowing through a vehicle is crucial for tracking system performance. The DT presented in this case will contain a user
interface (UI) allowing a user to view, save, and generate
reports of vehicle state data. Additionally, the UI will display
real-time vehicle state data, the projected driving path, and
detected object tracks in the vehicle’s proximity.
_2) Computation Offloading:_ A DT can alleviate the computational burden placed on resource-constrained on-board
hardware by allocating compute to better-suited equipment,
e.g., in the cloud. In this case study, we demonstrate this
functionality by offloading only the ACC system from the
vehicle to the DT. The ACC system determines the lead
vehicle, then computes a desired speed based on the distance
to the lead vehicle, finally sending the desired speed back to
the vehicle where the actuation is carried out.


(a) Base hardware: teleoperated vehicle retrofitted with advanced
sensor and control technology



(b) Main hardware layers of the physical twin: lower and upper level
chassis, and the autonomy elements



Fig. 1: The physical twin: a 1/10 [th] -scale autonomous vehicle



_3) Remote Management:_ The ability to remotely manage
ADAS systems enables the development, testing, and operation
of these systems from a different geographical location than
the physical twin. In addition to displaying real-time state data,
the UI will also enable the management and operation of ACC
remotely. This includes enabling/disabling ACC, setting the
desired speed, and sending an emergency braking command.
To implement these services, the DT must support low
end-to-end latency communication with the vehicle, contain
data collection and reporting capabilities, and more—all while
respecting the safety-critical nature of the system. For convenience and following community best practices, we refer
to the collection of the DT Entity, physical and middleware
components, and the UI as the digital twin system (DTS).
The users of our system can be grouped into two classes:
operators and developers. Operators use the DT to monitor
and remotely manage ACC, and developers use the DT to
troubleshoot during development using the data collection and
monitoring capabilities. In the _Liberty to act vs. Ability to act_
taxonomy from David and Bork [30], this DTS falls under the
Fully Autonomous Digital Twin, with the Human-in-the-Loop.


_B. Physical Twin_

UI will also enable the management and operation of ACC remotely... enabling/disabling ACC, setting the desired speed, and sending an emergency braking command', 'The users of our system can be grouped into two classes: operators and developers.'

The extraction accurately lists the key tools and enablers: low-latency communication link, cloud computing resources, UI, middleware, and notes that no specific simulation engine is named. It correctly omits naming a specific software platform as the text does not name one beyond middleware and hardware details. Quotes: 'To implement these services, the DT must support low end-to-end latency communication with the vehicle', 'offloading... compute to better-suited equipment, e.g., in the cloud', 'UI... allowing a user to view, save, and generate reports', 'Robot Operating System 2 (ROS2) as the middleware'. Note: The document mentions ROS2 and hardware components but does not name a specific simulation engine or platform, so the extraction is accurate.

offloading, devices must share the same notion of time.
Many standards mandate stricter clock synchronization
than 100 ms for safety-critical vehicular applications. We
require 100 ms accuracy due to hardware limitations.


_B. Architecture_


We draw on the ISO 23247 RA in the architectural design
phase. First, we identify key software (SW) components
(Tab. I) and their relationships to each other, and to the sensors
and actuators of the physical twin (Fig. 2).
Next, we map the SW components onto FEs and implement
them. To enable their execution on the vehicle, we implement
each FE as an individual ROS2 package. Each package contains one or more ROS2 nodes implementing the functionality
provided by the FE. This mapping helps organize the SW
components by the entity-based structure of ISO 23247.
User-facing components are organized into the _User Entity_,
within which the singular _User Interface FE_ is instantiated
in the _3D Visualization_ and _Control Panel_ components. SW
components that implement data management and control
mechanisms are organized into the _Digital Twin Entity_ . Data
pre-processing, actuation, and parts of the device communication functionality are organized into the _Physical Twin_
_Entity_ . The existence of this entity marks a departure from
the ISO 23247 RA. Most similar to the Physical Twin Entity
in the standard is the OME, which hosts resource-specific
functionality. By contrast, in our design, the Physical Twin
Entity reflects the inherent resources of the twinned system (e.g., compute, sensors, actuators) and can host general
automotive functionality, such as _Point Cloud Clustering &_
_Tracking_ . Considering that each entity has been deployed on
a different hardware component, such deviations from the
standard are substantial as they may impact the ownership of
the functionality and the engineering process of the DTS.
Not every ISO 23247 FE was required for our DTS. At the
same time, the _User Interface FE_ has been split into two subcomponents: visualization and control panel. Finally, one SW
component, _Data Storage_ did not have a naturally aligned FE.


**Physical Twin Entity*** **Digital Twin Entity** **User Entity**

In addition, a demonstration of the DT is publicly available. [2]


II. B ACKGROUND AND RELATED WORK


_A. The ISO 23247 standard and reference architecture_


In 2021, the International Standards Organization (ISO)
published the _ISO 23247 – Digital twin framework for manu-_
_facturing_ standard [10] that defines a development framework
for DTs. The standard is organized into four parts: 1) Overview
and general principles, 2) Reference architecture, 3) Digital
representation of physical manufacturing elements, and 4)
Information exchange. For our purposes, Part 2 is the most
relevant as it defines an entity-based RA, with each entity is
responsible for implementing a part of the DT’s functionality.
The _Observable Manufacturing Element (OME)_ is any item
that has an observable physical presence or operation in
manufacturing, e.g., equipment, facilities, products, personnel,
processes, etc. The _Data Collection and Device Control Entity_
is responsible for collecting data from, and controlling the
OME. The _Digital Twin Entity_ digitally represents the OME.
Finally, the _User Entity_ provides interfaces for users to interact
with the _Digital Twin Entity_, e.g., monitoring data or controlling the OME. Each entity is composed of _Functional Entities_
(FEs), which are smaller units responsible for a particular task
such as actuating the OME, transforming data into a humaninterpretable format, or interfacing with the user. The standard
does not give guidelines as to how FEs are to be implemented.
For example, one can choose to use OPC Unified Architecture

The description correctly identifies the instantiation of single ISO-23247 entities and the decentralized deployment on distinct hardware components. It accurately describes the splitting of the User Interface FE into two sub-FEs and notes the absence of hierarchical nesting.

The description accurately captures the ISO-23247 based entity decomposition, the implementation via ROS 2 packages and nodes, and the communication middleware. It correctly mentions the resource management strategy (mapping to dedicated compute) and the coordination mechanisms (publish/subscribe, services).

_A. The ISO 23247 standard and reference architecture_

Implementation of Services_ Below, we describe each DTS service as a composition of multiple SW components, summarized in Tab. II. All of the DTS services rely on the Data Translation component, which corresponds to the ISO 23247 Data Translation FE. This component serializes data, aligns message types, and performs unit conversions for every data stream in the system. Communication between SW components takes place within a ROS2 network, while inter-entity communication occurs over a Wi-Fi network using UDP in the transport layer. _1) Data Monitoring:_ To enable data monitoring, ACCrelevant data such as vehicle state information, object tracks, and projected driving path are displayed in real-time on the 3D Visualization, as shown in Fig. 3a. _A. Context_


DT technology enables an array of benefits for ACC through
off-loading any critical or non-critical computation for which
resource-constrained electronic control units (ECU) are typically not sufficient. To implement the ACC-DT, we develop
the following three services through a DT.
_1) Data Monitoring:_ Monitoring ACC-relevant data flowing through a vehicle is crucial for tracking system performance. The DT presented in this case will contain a user
interface (UI) allowing a user to view, save, and generate
reports of vehicle state data. Additionally, the UI will display
real-time vehicle state data, the projected driving path, and
detected object tracks in the vehicle’s proximity.
_2) Computation Offloading:_ A DT can alleviate the computational burden placed on resource-constrained on-board
hardware by allocating compute to better-suited equipment,
e.g., in the cloud. In this case study, we demonstrate this
functionality by offloading only the ACC system from the
vehicle to the DT. The ACC system determines the lead
vehicle, then computes a desired speed based on the distance
to the lead vehicle, finally sending the desired speed back to
the vehicle where the actuation is carried out.


(a) Base hardware: teleoperated vehicle retrofitted with advanced
sensor and control technology



(b) Main hardware layers of the physical twin: lower and upper level
chassis, and the autonomy elements

block_4_judge.reasoning: The extraction is highly detailed and matches the source text accurately. It correctly identifies the sensor types (LiDAR), the processing component ('Point Cloud Clustering & Tracking'), the specific actions of the Data Translation functional entity ('serializes data, aligns message types, and performs unit conversions'), and the network protocols ('ROS 2 network' for internal, 'Wi-Fi network' for external, though the source text truncates the transport protocol at 'U'). Supported by quotes: 'Point Cloud Clustering & Tracking', 'Data Translation component... serializes data, aligns message types, and performs unit conversions', 'Communication between SW components takes place within a ROS2 network', 'inter-entity communication occurs over a Wi-Fi network'.

The extraction accurately lists the specific remote management commands (ACC enable/disable, desired speed, emergency braking) and their transmission via the Data Translation component. It correctly describes the interaction with the vehicle's actuators. However, it attributes the generation of all commands to the 'Controlling/Actuation functional entities', whereas the source text specifies that the 'UI' (User Interface) implements the remote management commands (R5, III.A). Supported by quotes: 'UI will also enable the management and operation of ACC remotely... enabling/disabling ACC, setting the desired speed, and sending an emergency braking command', 'Actuation Converts desired speed into motor voltage commands', 'ACC Determines lead vehicle and computes following speed'.

The extraction provides a precise summary of the technical connection, identifying ROS 2 as the internal middleware and Wi-Fi as the external network. It correctly states the latency requirement of 100 ms and notes the absence of specific bandwidth figures. The client-server description is a fair interpretation of the vehicle/DTS roles described in the requirements and architecture. Supported by quotes: 'Communication between SW components takes place within a ROS2 network', 'inter-entity communication occurs over a Wi-Fi network', 'DTS shall maintain network latency of at most 100 ms'.

The extraction accurately reflects the limited information available in the text regarding hosting. It correctly cites the context of offloading compute to the 'cloud' and correctly identifies that specific details on deployment models (containers, VMs), scaling strategies, and availability guarantees are not present in the document. Supported by quotes: 'allocating compute to better-suited equipment, e.g., in the cloud'.
